message.content:
Domain Fronting is a technique where adversaries exploit Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) to obfuscate the true destination of HTTPS traffic. This is achieved by using different domain names in the Server Name Indication (SNI) field of the TLS header and the Host field of the HTTP header. If both domains are served by the same CDN, the CDN may route the traffic to the address specified in the HTTP header after unwrapping the TLS header. A variation, "domainless" fronting, uses a blank SNI field to bypass validation checks.

The potential impact includes evading network defenses and hiding Command and Control (C2) traffic. Common exploitation methods involve using tools like the meek plugin for Tor, Cobalt Strike, and Mythic, which support domain fronting to disguise C2 communications. Notable adversaries such as APT29 and SMOKEDHAM have utilized this technique.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring and analyzing traffic for mismatched SNI and HTTP Host fields, implementing strict SNI validation policies, and using network intrusion detection systems (NIDS) to detect anomalies. Additionally, organizations should work with CDN providers to ensure proper configuration and validation of domain fronting attempts.
